context:
Section 9 (Concluding remarks), page 33.

Second, we established weak duality in Theorem 2 only in case \(\mathcal E\) is super-linear. The case of a general non-linear expectation is difficult to analyze, and we only have a simple bound (20) for \(\mathcal E=\mathcal E^h\). Similarly, strong duality is only established for convex \(h\) and affine cost, and we wonder whether it also holds in more general situations.
title: Strong duality for distorted optimal transport beyond affine costs
background_latex:
- Let \((\Omega,\mathcal A,\mathbb P)\) be an atomless probability space and let \(\mathcal X\) be the set of bounded real-valued random variables on it.
- A distortion function is an increasing function \(h:[0,1]\to[0,1]\) with \(h(0)=0\) and \(h(1)=1\). The associated distorted expectation \(\mathbb E_h:\mathcal X\to\mathbb R\) is defined by
\[\mathbb E_h[Z]=\int_0^{\infty} h(\mathbb P(Z>x))\,dx+\int_{-\infty}^0\big(h(\mathbb P(Z>x))-1\big)\,dx,\qquad Z\in\mathcal X.\]
- Let \(\mu,\nu\) be probability measures on \(\mathbb R\). Write \(X\sim\mu\) and \(Y\sim\nu\) to mean that random variables \(X,Y\in\mathcal X\) have laws \(\mu,\nu\), respectively.
- Let \(c:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R\) be a measurable cost function such that \(c(X,Y)\in\mathcal X\) for all bounded \(X,Y\).
- Define the primal distorted optimal transport value
\[P_h(c;\mu,\nu)=\inf\big\{\mathbb E_h[c(X,Y)]: X\sim\mu,\ Y\sim\nu\big\}.\]
- Define the set of admissible dual pairs
\[S_c=\{(\varphi,\psi): \varphi(x)+\psi(y)\le c(x,y)\ \text{for all }x,y\in\mathbb R\},\]
where \(\varphi,\psi:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) are measurable and such that \(\varphi(X_\mu),\psi(X_\nu)\in\mathcal X\) for \(X_\mu\sim\mu\), \(X_\nu\sim\nu\).
- Define the (distorted) dual value
\[D_h(c;\mu,\nu)=\sup_{(\varphi,\psi)\in S_c}\Big\{\mathbb E_h[\varphi(X_\mu)]+\mathbb E_h[\psi(X_\nu)]\Big\}.\]
- The paper proves strong duality \(P_h(c;\mu,\nu)=D_h(c;\mu,\nu)\) when \(c\) is affine and \(h\) is any distortion function (Theorem 2), and only weak duality in broader settings.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine conditions on the cost function \(c\) and the distortion function \(h\) (beyond the case that \(c\) is affine and \(h\) is convex) under which strong duality holds for distorted optimal transport, i.e.,
\[P_h(c;\mu,
u)=D_h(c;\mu,
u)\quad	ext{for all probability measures }\mu,
u	ext{ on }\mathbb R.\]
In particular, either:
- Prove strong duality for a substantially larger class of non-affine costs \(c\) and/or non-convex distortions \(h\), or
- Give counterexamples showing that \(P_h(c;\mu,
u)>D_h(c;\mu,
u)\) can occur under specific, clearly stated regularity assumptions on \(c\) and \(h\).

Determine conditions on the cost function \(c\) and the distortion function \(h\) (beyond the case that \(c\) is affine and \(h\) is convex) under which strong duality holds for distorted optimal transport, i.e.,
\[P_h(c;\mu,\nu)=D_h(c;\mu,\nu)\quad\text{for all probability measures }\mu,\nu\text{ on }\mathbb R.\]
In particular, either:
- Prove strong duality for a substantially larger class of non-affine costs \(c\) and/or non-convex distortions \(h\), or
- Give counterexamples showing that \(P_h(c;\mu,\nu)>D_h(c;\mu,\nu)\) can occur under specific, clearly stated regularity assumptions on \(c\) and \(h\).

Similarly, strong duality is only established for convex \(h\) and affine cost, and we wonder whether it also holds in more general situations.
subject_classification: Optimal Transport and Duality in Mathematical Optimization
